questions.question: Define Public Cloud Platform and name the three major public cloud providers in the industry.
questions.hint: Focus on the definition and the three major platform providers discussed in the module
keyPoints: Public Cloud Platform definition: on-demand computing resources over the internet by third-party providers
Three major providers: Google, Amazon, Microsoft
Specific names: Google App Engine (GAE), Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure
Key characteristic: pay-as-you-go model
Resources managed by provider, accessed via internet

questions.question: Explain the key differences between Platform as a Service (Paas) and Infrastructure as a Service (Iaas) with one example of each.
questions.hint: Use AWS EC2 as IaaS example and GAE as PaaS example
keyPoints: IaaS: provides virtualized computing resources (servers, storage, networking)
PaaS: provides complete development and deployment platform
Management responsibility: IaaS - user manages OS, runtime; PaaS - provider manages everything except code
Example IaaS: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines
Example PaaS: Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service
Flexibility vs simplicity tradeoff

questions.question: What is Google App Engine (GAE)? Explain how it provides a fully managed PaaS environment.
questions.hint: Focus on the abstraction of infrastructure management
keyPoints: GAE is Google's PaaS offering launched in 2008
Fully managed: no server management required
Developers upload application code only
Google handles: provisioning, scaling, load balancing, health management
Automatic scaling based on traffic
Built-in services: Datastore, Memcache, Task Queues

questions.question: Differentiate between Google App Engine Standard Environment and Flexible Environment.
questions.hint: Compare based on container technology, scaling behavior, and cost model
keyPoints: Standard Environment: container-based, runs in sandbox, limited language support
Flexible Environment: uses Docker containers, supports custom runtimes
Scaling: Standard - automatic to zero; Flexible - automatic but minimum instances
Instance startup: Standard - seconds; Flexible - minutes
Cost: Standard - free tier available; Flexible - pay for running instances
Network access: Standard - restricted; Flexible - full network access

questions.question: List and briefly explain four key services provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS).
questions.hint: Mention both compute and storage services
keyPoints: EC2: Elastic Compute Cloud - virtual servers in the cloud
S3: Simple Storage Service - object storage service
EBS: Elastic Block Store - block storage for EC2
VPC: Virtual Private Cloud - isolated cloud network
RDS: Relational Database Service - managed database
Lambda: Serverless compute service

questions.question: What is Microsoft Azure App Service? Mention its key features.
questions.hint: Focus on PaaS aspects and language support
keyPoints: Azure App Service is Microsoft's PaaS offering
Features: fully managed platform for building web apps
Supports multiple languages: .NET, Java, Node.js, Python, PHP
Automatic scaling capabilities
Built-in DevOps support with CI/CD
High availability and enterprise security
Supports web apps, mobile app backends, and API apps

questions.question: Explain the architecture of Google App Engine (GAE) including its application lifecycle, automatic scaling mechanism, and built-in services.
questions.hint: Draw a diagram showing the architecture components and explain each
keyPoints: Architecture components: Frontend, Application Server, Datastore, Memcache, Task Queue
Application lifecycle: developer uploads code -> GAE distributes to instances
Request routing via Frontend to available instances
Automatic scaling: scales up with traffic, scales down to zero when idle
Load balancing: distributes traffic across instances
Built-in services: Datastore (NoSQL), Memcache (caching), Task Queues (async tasks)
Health management: automatic instance recovery
Versions: support for multiple versions, traffic splitting

questions.question: Compare Google App Engine (GAE), Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Microsoft Azure in terms of service models, compute offerings, and ideal use cases.
questions.hint: Create a comparison table covering service models, services, and use cases
keyPoints: Service models: AWS (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), Azure (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), GAE (PaaS primarily)
Compute offerings: AWS - EC2, Lambda, Elastic Beanstalk; Azure - VMs, Azure Functions, App Service; GAE - Standard/Flexible environments
Storage: AWS - S3, EBS; Azure - Blob Storage; GAE - Datastore
Database: AWS - RDS, DynamoDB; Azure - SQL Database, Cosmos DB; GAE - Cloud SQL, Datastore
Use cases: GAE - simple web apps, startups; AWS - enterprise, complex architectures; Azure - Windows-based apps, enterprise Microsoft integration
Learning curve: GAE simplest, AWS steepest, Azure moderate

questions.question: Explain AWS EC2 and S3 in detail, including their features, instance types, and common use cases.
questions.hint: Cover both compute and storage aspects with specific features
keyPoints: EC2: Elastic Compute Cloud - resizable virtual servers
Instance families: General purpose, Compute optimized, Memory optimized, Storage optimized, GPU instances
EC2 features: Elastic IP, Auto Scaling, Load Balancing, Security Groups
S3: Simple Storage Service - object storage
S3 features: 99.999999999% durability, versioning, lifecycle policies, bucket policies
EC2 use cases: web servers, applications, databases, batch processing
S3 use cases: backup and storage, data lakes, static website hosting, content delivery

questions.question: Compare the PaaS offerings across the three platforms: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, and Google App Engine.
questions.hint: Focus on the level of abstraction and management responsibility for each
keyPoints: AWS Elastic Beanstalk: deploys and scales web apps, supports multiple languages, user controls infrastructure
Azure App Service: builds web apps, supports .NET, Java, Node.js, integrated with Visual Studio
Google App Engine: fully managed, strict sandbox in Standard, container-based in Flexible
Management level: Beanstalk - user has control over environment; App Service - balanced; GAE - least control
Scaling: All support automatic scaling but with different configurations
Pricing: All offer free tiers; Beanstalk and App Service charge for underlying resources; GAE has specific pricing models
Vendor lock-in: Highest in GAE, medium in App Service, lowest in Beanstalk

questions.question: Design a cloud architecture for a simple e-commerce web application using any one public cloud platform. Justify your choice of services.
questions.hint: Select one platform and justify each component choice
keyPoints: Choose any platform (AWS/Azure/GAE) and justify
Frontend: CDN for static content delivery
Compute: Load balancer + auto-scaling compute instances/App Service
Database: Managed database service (RDS/SQL Database/Cloud SQL)
Storage: Object storage for product images
Caching: In-memory cache for session data
Security: Firewall rules, SSL/TLS certificates
Monitoring and logging for performance tracking
Justification: scalability, cost, managed services

questions.question: What are the advantages of using a Public Cloud Platform for business applications?
questions.hint: Focus on business and economic benefits
keyPoints: No upfront capital expenditure (pay-as-you-go)
Eliminates infrastructure management burden
Global infrastructure with high availability
Automatic scaling based on demand
Disaster recovery and business continuity built-in
Access to enterprise-grade technology
Faster time to market
Pay only for what you use

questions.question: Explain the concept of Inter-Cloud Resource Management and its importance in cloud computing.
questions.hint: Focus on multi-cloud strategy benefits and challenges
keyPoints: Inter-Cloud: management of resources across multiple cloud providers
Enables workload portability between clouds
Prevents vendor lock-in
Provides disaster recovery across clouds
Optimizes cost by leveraging spot/preemptible instances
Challenges: standardized APIs, data migration, security
Tools: Kubernetes for container orchestration across clouds
